coredata.dc:title: Core-shell polyaniline functionalized carbon quantum dots for supercapacitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Kakaei, Karim
Khodadoost, Somayyeh
Gholipour, Maryam
Shouraei, Nazila
coredata.dc:description: A green electrochemical approach is developed for the fabrication of polyaniline (PANI) nanofiber and carbon quantum dots (C-dots) nanoparticles. In this regard, C-dots were manufactured by DC power supply by 2 Pt plates in ethanol and NaOH solution. While, PANI was synthesized by chronopotentiometry using a 3-electrode compartment system in aniline and SDS solution. The C-dots/PANI composite was prepared via adsorption of C-dots on PANI to be produced PANI as a core and C-dots as a shell, due to the electrostatic adsorption of carboxylic groups of C-dots on PANI. The acquired composite material shows good performance for supercapacitors with a high specific capacity of 264.6 F g−1 at a current density of 2.5 A g−1. The long term studies conducted on materials show that the composite based on (C-dot/PANI) remain high stability up to the tested 5000 charge–discharge cycles. The results from double-layer capacitance, cyclic voltammetry, and the impedance test display that the addition of C-dots to the PANI matrix can enhance the charge transport manner, which leads to higher supercapacitor property of the composite materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyaniline
Carbon quantum dots
Core-shell
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photograph image of C-dot image by the electrochemical method using two Pt electrodes in ethanol/NaOH at different times.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis procedure of C-dot/PANI composite.
(a) SEM image of the C-dot. (b) SEM image of the C-dot/PANI. (c) TEM image of the C-dot (d) TEM image of the C-dot/PANI.
FT-IR spectra of C-dots, pure PANI and C-dot/PANI.
CV profiles of C-dot recorded at different rates for in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution between (0 to −0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl).
CV profiles of C-dot/PANI recorded at dissimilar rates in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution between (0 to −0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl).
Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles after 5000 cycles of C-dot/PANI using in 1 MH 2SO4 solution at various current densities.
Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles after 5000 cycles of C-dot, in 1 MH 2SO4 solution at various current densities.
Nyquist diagrams of complex-plane impedance recorded before and after CV in 1 MH 2SO4 solution using a three-electrode configuration for C-dot.
Nyquist diagrams of complex-plane impedance recorded before and after CV in 1 MH 2SO4 solution by using a three-electrode configuration for C-dot/PANI.

head.ce:title: Core-shell polyaniline functionalized carbon quantum dots for supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O. Box. 55136-553, Maragheh, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University Of Maragheh, P.O. Box. 55136-553, Maragheh, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green electrochemical approach is developed for the fabrication of polyaniline (PANI) nanofiber and carbon quantum dots (C-dots) nanoparticles. In this regard, C-dots were manufactured by DC power supply by 2 Pt plates in ethanol and NaOH solution. While, PANI was synthesized by chronopotentiometry using a 3-electrode compartment system in aniline and SDS solution. The C-dots/PANI composite was prepared via adsorption of C-dots on PANI to be produced PANI as a core and C-dots as a shell, due to the electrostatic adsorption of carboxylic groups of C-dots on PANI. The acquired composite material shows good performance for supercapacitors with a high specific capacity of 264.6 F g−1 at a current density of 2.5 A g−1. The long term studies conducted on materials show that the composite based on (C-dot/PANI) remain high stability up to the tested 5000 charge–discharge cycles. The results from double-layer capacitance, cyclic voltammetry, and the impedance test display that the addition of C-dots to the PANI matrix can enhance the charge transport manner, which leads to higher supercapacitor property of the composite materials.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of energy storage, supercapacitors are now extremely promising device due to their unique advantages such as high power density, long cycle life, reversibility, and small environmental impact. Usually, the supercapacitor performance is mostly determined by the materials of electrode. For example, metal oxides, conductive polymers, and carbonaceous are considered as the most important classes of supercapacitor materials. Therein, different materials have been used as electrode supports including activated carbon [1], amorphous carbons [2], carbon nanotubes [3], graphene [4,5], metal oxides such as manganese dioxide [6], ruthenium dioxide [7], cobalt oxide and sulfide [8], nickel oxide (NiO); and conducting polymers for example polyaniline (PANI) [9] and polypyrrole [10]. However, the limitation of ion accessibility and diffusibility of many carbonaceous or metal oxides and poor electrical conductivity of metal oxides and conductive polymer are the most obstructions for these materials to use as energy storage individually.

In the group of conductive polymers, PANI has the highest specific capacitance, good stability, high conductivity, low cost, fast reversible doping-dedoping interactions between the PANI and the electrolytes, easy synthesis, and extender of the accessible surface [11]. However, its poor mechanical and cycling stability due to repeated volumetric swelling/shrinking of the polymer during charge/discharge processes are the major obstacles to its practical applications in energy storage [12]. In order to improve the physicochemical possessions of PANI, carbonaceous based materials such as carbon nanotubes [13], mesoporous carbon [14], carbon fiber [15], and graphene [9,16] can modify polymers physical properties. They may perform the new features by producing a percolation network within the polymer [11]. Carbon-based materials can support the PANI skeleton by limiting its expansion and contraction to be used in the pseudocapacitance. On the other hand, the introduction of carbonaceous into PANI polymer can not only increase the conductivity, but also obtain the advantages of the two materials, demonstrating the higher electrochemical performance of the composite and synergistic effect between PANI and carbon materials.

Among different carbon allotropes, carbon quantum dot has not received much attention for use in supercapacitors. Recently, carbon quantum dots (C-dots), an emerging class within the carbon allotrope family, are a candidate as promising agents for the several application such as photoelectrochemical [17], methanol oxidation [18], oxygen reduction reaction [19,20], methanol tolerance [21] and optical bioimaging [22]. Therefore, properties such as physical, chemical, and optical of carbon based materials can be derived from synthetic methods, which may be used in various applications. For example, bare C-dots or doped- C-dots can be synthesized by using carbon sources such as Citric acid and cystamine dihydrochloride, Citric acid and thiamine hydrochloride, Feathers, Egg white, Egg yolk, Glutathione, Grass, Glucose and urea, Folic acid, Garlic, etc. via hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, and electrochemical methods [19,21,23–30].

C-dots are novel zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based materials famous for their small size (2 nm–10 nm) and relatively strong fluorescence properties. Due to the existence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups, as well as strong electrostatic interaction and p–p conjugation, various molecules such as biomolecules, organic molecules, polymers, and nanoparticles can be linked to the surface of C- dots. Thus, different electrodes are fabricated as the signal probes in to be used in the sensors [31–33]. For example, the fabricated composite of RGO/C-dot with a 2:1 mass ratio revealed high specific capacitance with maximum capacitances of 278 F g−1 and 227 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 and 2 mV s−1 [34]. Sahoo et, al. designed a novel material for supercapacitor by using NiS based C-dot. The composite material showed good efficiancy for supercapacitors with a specific capacity of 880 F g−1 at a current density of 2 A g−1 [35]. Genc et al. [36] fabricated a novel hybrid supercapacitor by α-MnO2 nanorods and C-dots from degassed Cola-drink and rGO to be used in supercapacitor [37]. They obtained a high capacitance value of 17.3 μFcm−2, which is attributed to the quality of electrode materials.

In this study, EtOH and NaOH were used to prepare C-dots as well as aniline and SDS for PANI by in-situ electrochemical technique. The as-synthesized C-dotes was added to PANI through adsorption to be fabricated PANI as a core and C-dots as a shell. This novel composite provides the specific capacitance as high as 264.6 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1.

A three-electrode compartment system consisted of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), platinum plate (Pt) and an Ag/AgCl (saturated) were used for electrochemical production of PANI and also for electrochemical analysis. The GCE, Pt and Ag/AgCl electrodes are applied as working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The synthesis of C-dots was performed using two platinum electrodes.

The electro-synthesis of C-dots was carried out in a two-electrode structure. The electrolyte of the electrochemical process was prepared by mixing ethanol/H2O (100 mL; volume ratio = 99.5:0.5) with a suitable amount (0.2–0.5 g) of NaOH, by using two platinum plates at 30ᵒC for 4 h and DC power supply (3V). Fig. 1 shows the C-dot photograph synthesis by electrochemical technique at various times. As shown in Fig. 1 the color of the solution changes from bright red at 0.5 h to dark red at 4 h, due to the increases of C-dot at long time.

The C-dots/PANI was synthesized by distillated aniline and obtained C-dot as described above. For this purpose, we synthesized PANI as published previously and describes here briefly [9]. The PANI nanofilms were manufactured by electropolymerization of aniline onto the surface of a carbon paper support in a cell containing a solution of 32 mM aniline (Merck) and 40 mM sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). Then, PANI has been synthesized with a current strength in the range of 0.3–0.5 mA/cm2 for 2 h. Finally, the obtained C-dots at 4 h (2 cc) were added to the PANI surface to be adsorbed and changed from red to colorless (C-dots/PANI).

A schematic diagram of the preparation way of the C-dot/PANI as a core-shell is shown in Fig. 2 . In the adsorption process, the color of electrochemically produced C-dots in alkaline ethanol gradually changed from red to colorless. The achieved C-dots in NaOH solution contained a large amount of –COO- active moieties groups on their faces. When C-dots were added into the PANI doped SDS dispersion, they were preferentially adsorbed on the surface of C-dots as a result of electrostatic adsorption and interaction, between C–N•+ of PANI and –COO- of C-dot, due to the exchange of dodecyl sulfate by carboxylic groups on quantum dot.

SEM and TEM images of C-dot and C-dot/PANI samples are shown in Fig. 3 . The SEM image of C-dots shows a more compact structure (Fig. 3a). While, SEM image of three-dimensional C-dot/PANI displayed several distributed pores from 200 nm to 50 nm (Fig. 3b). TEM image of pure C-dot (Fig. 3c) demonstrates that the spherical shapes of C-dot are well uniformly dispersed and in the range of 2.5–4 nm. Fig. 3d shows the TEM image of the C-dot/PANI sample. The PANI polymer forms separate island-like matrices with C-dots inserted in it. On the other hand, PANI nanosheets have been located at the core of the composite and C-dots as shell deposited on it.

The substituents parts on the C-dots, PANI, and C-dot/PANI samples were confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 4 ). In C-dot spectrum, the broad C–OH peak at around 3500 cm−1 could be seen. The doublet peaks at 1620 and 1670 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibration of –C = in -COO- and –COOH, respectively. As shown in FTIR of C-dot/PANI, after being adsorbed C-dot on PANI, the C-dots signals were concealed. The broad peaks at 3480 cm−1 in FTIR of C-dot/PANI and PANI were attributed to the coupling of O–H and N–H stretching frequency, respectively. Two sharp peaks at 1600 and 1555 cm−1 in C-dot/PANI and PANI can be associated to quinoid and benzenoid ring

Structures of PANI, respectively [9]. In C-dot/PANI and PANI A band at aroun 1300 cm−1 is attributed to the CN+ stretching adjacent to the quinoid structure while a medium intensity band at 1210 cm−1 is assigned with C–N stretching vibration in the alternate units of quinoid–benzenoid–quinoid rings.

Figs. 5 and 6
indicate the CV response of the supercapacitor cells based on C-dot and C-dot/PANI at applying speed of 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mV s−1, respectively. Reasonable charge propagations at the low scan rate of the CV curve of the electrodes are reveled and show rectangular shape as the EDLC mechanism. By increasing up to 50 mV s−1, the CV profile of both samples becomes more oblique. By integrating the area under the charge curve, the composite electrode shows a much more specific capacitance than that bare C-dot material. By comparing the area under the CV image, the capacitance of both samples was calculated. The resulted data shows that the C-dot/PANI has larger charge area than that of the C-dot electrode, indicating higher specific capacitance.

Galvanostatic charging/discharging test was applied to assess the specific capacitance of C-dot and C-dot/PANI. The charge/discharge characteristics of pure C-dot and C-dot/PANI electrodes are shown in Figs. 7 and 8
at several currents from 2.5A/g to 6.5 A/g. The chemical state of oxygen functional groups on C-dot particles may be led to a non-perfect symmetric triangle feature in the charge/discharge profile of these samples, representing a small pseudo-capacitance besides the EDL capacitance. Moreover, no noticeable IR drop in the discharge curve was observed due to minimum internal resistance, demonstrating less energy wasted to produce undesired heat through charge/discharge procedures [38].

It should be noted the duration of charge-discharge in C-dot/PANI is higher than that C-dot, suggesting the higher specific capacitance for C-dot/PANI. This value is corresponding to the values obtained from the CV observations. The calculated Specific capacitances from the discharge curves at 2.5 A g−1 current density for pure C-dot and C-dot/PANI electrodes were 69.3 and 264.6 F g−1. The C-dot/PANI capacitance is comparable with reported for pure C-dot electrode (∼220 F g−1) and pure PANI (350 Fg-1) at 1 A/g [9,39]. The C-dot/PANI provides a distinctive electrode by combining the electrochemical synthesis of C-dot and PANI polymer. This can maximize the contact of the surfaces and electrolyte via oxygen functional groups to increase the specific capacitance. To decrease the band gap of the PANI electrode, incorporation of C-dot nanoparticles into PANI nanostructures can alert the values of LUMO and HOMO and improve the electron mobility. Then the electron work function at the carbon/liquid interface can decrease compared to pure PANI similar to previously reported about graphene and PANI [9]. The charge layer and solvent layer can share the potential of a double layer, and then the sample capacitance is offered by these two aspects. In this view, the enhancement of mobility in the sample would improve from a more substantial space charge capacitance. Therefore, the introduction of a C-dot in PANI is an essential key factor for attaining a large specific capacitance.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) plays a crucial role in the study of charge transfer through the electron-solution interface. Figs.9 and 10
show the EIS of the C-dot and C-dot/PANI electrodes measured over a range of applied frequency from 100 kHz to 100 mHz at open circuit voltage with an ac perturbation of 5 mV. Moreover, the EIS curves were recorded before CV and after CV aging 1000 cycles at 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution (50 mV s−1). The impedance diagrams of the samples before and after CV have similar characteristics. There are two outstanding areas, including the charge process resistance at high frequencies and the mass transfer processes at low frequencies. Before cycling, in the C-dot electrode, at the low frequency, a pure capacitive characteristics can be seen as its plot consist of the vertical lines close to 45ᵒ, demonstrating lower ion diffusion resistance in the structure of the electrodes [40–42]. While, the C-dot/PANI electrode exhibits more vertical line than C-dot, which correspond to an ideal capacitor and a shorter diffusion region of ions in the electrode surface, which could simplify the efficient accessibility of electrolyte ions in the near of C-dot/PANI. At high frequency zone, a moderately lower diameter of C-dot/PANI than C-dot related to a smaller charge transfer resistance (Rct) affected by the Faradic responses and the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) on the interface.

A simple producer approach of the adsorption of electro-synthetic C-dots on PANI surface via electrostatic interaction to obtain core−shell C-dots/PANI composite with enhanced capacitance performance was demonstrated. The incorporating of C-dots into PANI nanofiber may have distinctive electrical properties and potential applications, such as electrode capacitor materials with high-efficiency.

Various characterization techniques, including SEM, TEM, and FTIR have been used to study the electrode material properties. In electrochemical tests, C-dots/PANI shows the specific capacitance of 264.6 F g−1 from charge/discharge profile, which is higher than that of pure C-dots material (69.3 F g−1) at 2.5 Ag-1. Therefore, the C-dots/PANI with excellent cycling stability and high specific capacity are entirely appropriate and hopeful electrode materials for high performance supercapacitors.

Karim Kakaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Supervision. Somayyeh Khodadoost: Investigation, Formal analysis. Maryam Gholipour: Writing - review & editing. Nazila Shouraei: Software, Writing - review & editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpcs.2020.109753.

Our ongoing studies focuse on effective electrochemical methods for exfoliated graphite (EG) electrode and carbon quantum dots (Carbon 51 (2013), 195–201, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 2 (37), 15428–15436 and …) and straightforward Core-shell polyaniline functionalized carbon quantum dots to be used in the supercapacitor. The results presented in the submitted manuscript showed that a single-step synthesized polyaniline and carbon quantum dot by electrochemical method via adsorption of C-dots on PANI to be produced PANI as a core and C-dots as a shell. The composite of C-dots and PANI shows superior capacitive behaviors.

It has been shown for the first time that C-dots and PANI exhibits the specific capacitance of 264.6 F g−1 at 2.5 A/g from charge/discharge profile, which is very higher than that of pure C-dots and PANI materials and also higher than that composite which reported in literature.

Also, the obtained results showed that the method of C-dots/PANI composite could be considered for practical application such as gas diffusion electrode and supercapacitor because of possible reduction of the material costs.
We would be very grateful to you for considering this manuscript for publication in Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids.
We look forward your positive response.
